title: Arithmetic and geometry of Picard modular surfaces
speaker: Dinakar Ramakrishnan
affiliation: California Institute of Technology; Visitor, School of Mathematics
abstract: Of interest are (i) the conjecture of Bombieri (and Lang) that for any smooth projective surface $X$ of general type over a number field $k$, the set $X(k)$, of $k$-rational points is not Zariski dense, and (ii) the conjecture of Lang that $X(k)$, is even finite if in addition $X$ is hyperbolic, i.e., there is no non-constant holomorphic map from the complex line $C$ into $X(C)$. We can verify them for the Picard modular surfaces $X$ which are smooth toroidal compactifications of congruence quotients $Y$ of the unit ball in $\mathbb C^2$.

proof_stages.label: Diophantine motivation and conjectural geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames Picard modular surfaces through Faltings' finiteness theorem, the relation between general type, hyperbolicity and ball uniformization, and the Bombieri--Lang and Lang conjectures for rational points.

proof_stages.label: Mazur's modular-curve template
proof_stages.summary: Mazur's proof for X_0(n) is presented as the guiding model: map the curve to a finite Mordell--Weil quotient of its Jacobian and use a formal-immersion criterion at the cusp, detected by the first q-expansion coefficient.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Picard modular surfaces and compactifications
proof_stages.summary: The relevant surfaces are set up as ball quotients B_2/Gamma with toroidal compactifications whose boundary consists of elliptic curves, and the arithmetic subgroups are described via unitary groups over CM fields with fields of definition for congruence levels.

proof_stages.label: Albanese reduction and Faltings on abelian varieties
proof_stages.summary: For arithmetic Gamma with X_Gamma of general type, enough H^1 classes give a sufficiently large Albanese variety, and Faltings' theorem on subvarieties of abelian varieties confines rational points in the Albanese image to finitely many translates, hence to a non-dense exceptional locus on X_Gamma.

proof_stages.label: Elimination of contracted curves and boundary contributions
proof_stages.summary: Curves in the hyperbolic open surface contracted by the Albanese map have genus at least two and are handled by Faltings' curve theorem, while Nadel's curvature theorem forces low-genus curves meeting the toroidal boundary to lie in the boundary, giving finiteness of Y_Gamma(k').

proof_stages.label: Prospective Mazur-style formal immersion for Picard surfaces
proof_stages.summary: For congruence Picard modular surfaces the speaker sketches ongoing work constructing finite Mordell--Weil quotients from residual Picard modular forms and reducing the needed formal immersion at a cusp to maximal rank of a matrix of Fourier--Jacobi coefficients modulo p.

examples.name: Uniform boundedness of torsion for elliptic curves over Q
examples.note: Mazur's determination of rational points on X_0(n) is cited as yielding finitely many possibilities for E(Q)_tors, illustrating the Diophantine strength of the modular-curve method.
examples.engagement_reason: he not only states Mazur’s uniform boundedness corollary, but sketches the modular-curve argument via X0(n), formal immersions, reduction, and class-number obstruction leading to Y0(n)(Q) being empty for large n

examples.name: Elliptic curves on U(2,1) surfaces
examples.note: The existence of elliptic curves on Picard modular surfaces is used to make concrete why a variety of general type need not be hyperbolic, motivating the distinction between Bombieri--Lang and Lang finiteness statements.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the elliptic curves appearing on Picard/U(2,1) modular surface compactifications, using cusp elliptic curves, boundary divisors, Nadel’s theorem, torsion points, and Fourier–Jacobi expansions along the elliptic curve

passes.reason: Genus-one curves are boundary low-genus cases and are cited as obstructions showing general-type U(2,1) surfaces need not be hyperbolic.
Elliptic curves are genus-one boundary cases for hyperbolicity and are used here as curves lying on U(2,1) surfaces to show general type need not imply hyperbolic.
Genus-one curves are borderline non-hyperbolic subcurves, used to show that a U(2,1) general-type surface need not be hyperbolic.
